title: Eviction Timeline & Attorney Fees 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate eviction timeline and attorney fees by case type. Free tool for forecasting legal costs and lawsuit duration.
intro: The US rental market is governed by state and local laws with specific eviction procedures and timelines. Attorney fees vary by jurisdiction, case type (non-payment, lease expiration, breach), and cost of living. This calculator provides typical timelines and fee estimates updated for 2026, reviewed regularly to keep you informed.
keyTakeaway: **Typical timeline + attorney fees**. Direct calculation based on case type. Get estimates for non-payment, lease expiration, and breach evictions with legal cost projections.
useCases: Eviction timeline planning for landlords and property managers
Legal professionals and real estate students
Validate forecasted costs before legal proceedings
Quick reference for case duration and fee estimation
explanation:
The **eviction process** in the US is governed by state law, with variations in procedures, notice periods, and timelines. Common grounds include non-payment of rent, lease expiration, and lease violations.

## How to Calculate

This calculator estimates typical eviction timelines and attorney fees based on case type. It applies standard formulas with your inputs, checking against typical ranges and flagging unusual values.

## Important Notes

This is an informational estimate only. For critical decisions involving legal matters, consult a licensed attorney in your jurisdiction. Timelines and fees are current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically for accuracy.

faq.q: How long does an eviction lawsuit typically take?
faq.a: Timeline varies by jurisdiction and case type, ranging from 3-18 months. Non-payment cases may be faster in some states; lease violations typically take longer.

faq.q: What are typical attorney fees for eviction?
faq.a: Fees range 15-25% of annual rent or $500-$3,000 depending on jurisdiction and case complexity. Flat fees and hourly rates vary by state and attorney.

faq.q: What notice must a landlord give before evicting for non-payment?
faq.a: Typically 3-30 days depending on state law. Most states require written notice with specific formatting, and some require proof of delivery.

faq.q: Can a tenant delay or stop an eviction?
faq.a: Possibly, through legal defense, paying back rent before judgment, filing for bankruptcy, or requesting continuances. Consult a lawyer for specific options.

faq.q: Do I need an attorney to file for eviction?
faq.a: While not always legally required, hiring an attorney increases chances of success and ensures proper procedure, which varies significantly by state.

faq.q: What are the main grounds for eviction?
faq.a: Common grounds: non-payment of rent, lease violation (noise, unauthorized occupants, pets), lease expiration, and illegal activity.

faq.q: How much does the entire eviction process cost?
faq.a: Total costs typically range $1,500-$5,000+ including court filing fees ($50-$500), attorney fees, sheriff service, and other expenses.

faq.q: What happens after the eviction judgment is entered?
faq.a: If tenant doesn't vacate voluntarily, landlord requests a writ of eviction. Sheriff or constable physically removes tenant within a specified period.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, does not require external data updates
